title: Yaki Udon

overview.description: A Japanese stir-fried noodle dish made with thick, chewy udon noodles, chicken and vegetables in a savoury soy-based sauce.

method: Mix all the sauce ingredients together in a small bowl.
If using vacuum-packed udon, loosen the noodles according to the packet instructions.
If using frozen udon, briefly boil or steam until loosened, then drain.
Heat a wok or large frying pan over medium-high heat.
Add the chicken and stir-fry until lightly browned and cooked through.
Remove the chicken and set aside.
Add the onion, carrots, cabbage and mushrooms to the wok.
Stir-fry until the vegetables are beginning to soften but still retain some texture.
Return the chicken to the wok.
Toss everything together until the noodles are hot and coated in the sauce.

learning.did-you-know: Yaki means grilled or fried, so yaki udon simply means fried udon noodles. Unlike ramen or other noodle soups, the noodles are stir-fried with the other ingredients.
learning.what-you-will-learn: How noodle thickness and texture affect stir-frying, and how a simple Japanese sauce can balance salty, sweet and savoury flavours.

notes.why-its-in-the-chapman-kitchen: A Japanese noodle dish that introduces a completely different noodle texture from the egg noodles used in chow mein, mee goreng and mie goreng Jawa.
chapman-tips: Don't boil the udon for longer than necessary; it only needs to be loosened before stir-frying.
The thick noodles can absorb a surprising amount of sauce.
Cut the vegetables fairly thinly so they cook quickly while retaining some crunch.
Keep the wok hot but avoid stirring so aggressively that the udon breaks apart.
The sauce should coat the noodles rather than pool in the bottom of the pan.
Chicken thigh is more forgiving than breast in a fast stir-fry.
For a vegetarian version, replace the chicken with mushrooms, tofu or more vegetables and use a vegetarian oyster sauce.
notes.batch-cooking: Best eaten fresh. The udon can become soft and sticky after freezing and reheating.
notes.leftovers: Reheat quickly in a hot frying pan or wok with a splash of water. Leftovers can make a good packed lunch.
